title: ICMP and UDP filtering at customer edge causes DoT/DoH fallback loop and intermittent gRPC streaming stalls on private deploy
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent model artifact download failures and GRPC streaming resets from their private deployment. Failures cluster around bulk inference jobs and long-lived streams.

Impact:
- Multiple inference requests returning 502/504 or streaming disconnects for a subset of serving nodes for ~10-30s every 5-20 minutes.
- High error rates during model pulls (~10-15% of downloads) when scale-up events occur.
- Customer workload (batch scoring) experiencing timeouts and downstream job failures.

Environment:
- Private deployment (customer-managed VPC) peered with Redwood control plane.
- Edge: Palo Alto SD-WAN appliance + corporate firewall policy.
- Observed region: prod/us-east-1.

Steps to reproduce (observed pattern):
1) Trigger parallel model pulls by scaling serving nodes (e.g., deploy new model or scale replica count).
2) Start long-lived streaming sessions to same serving nodes (chat/stream).
3) After ~1-2 minutes of sustained parallel activity, some streams drop and artifact downloads stall/retry.

Observed behavior:
- tcpdump on serving node shows repeated ICMP \"fragmentation needed\" messages followed by apparent UDP 53 timeouts.
- Local resolver on customer forwarder attempts DoH (443) then DoT (853) then plain UDP 53 in fallback order; when UDP 53 is blocked the resolver retries DoT/DoH but the corporate TLS inspection rewrites SNI causing TLS handshake failures.
- Control-plane auth calls timeout intermittently; serving nodes enter backoff and retry model fetches, causing 503s at client side.
- GRPC streams see RST/GoAway events aligned with the time windows of resolver churn and PMTU probes.

Expected behavior:
- DNS resolution and artifact fetches should succeed without blackholing during PMTU discovery or DoH/DoT fallback.

Logs / artifacts provided:
- tcpdump pcap from customer edge (attached in support portal) showing ICMP type 3 code 4 (frag needed) packets and subsequent retransmits.
- Resolver logs (unbound) showing repeated retries between DoH/DoT/UDP and TLS alert 40 (handshake failure) from inspection device.
- Serving node system logs showing repeated \"artifact fetch retry\" messages and gRPC stream reset counters increasing.

Customer-provided context:
- Firewall team recently deployed strict egress policy that blocks UDP 53 and rate-limits ICMP to 1 pps per prefix for security.
- SD-WAN vendor recently enabled MSS clamping on some tunnels.

Issue summary:
Customer reports intermittent model artifact download failures and GRPC streaming resets from their private deployment. Failures cluster around bulk inference jobs and long-lived streams.

Impact:
- Multiple inference requests returning 502/504 or streaming disconnects for a subset of serving nodes for ~10-30s every 5-20 minutes.

- tcpdump on serving node shows repeated ICMP \"fragmentation needed\" messages followed by apparent UDP 53 timeouts.
- Local resolver on customer forwarder attempts DoH (443) then DoT (853) then plain UDP 53 in fallback order; when UDP 53 is blocked the resolver retries DoT/DoH but the corporate TLS inspection rewrites SNI causing TLS handshake failures.
- Control-plane auth calls timeout intermittently; serving nodes enter backoff and retry model fetches, causing 503s at client side.
- GRPC streams see RST/GoAway events aligned with the time windows of resolver churn and PMTU probes.

Expected behavior:

Logs / artifacts provided:

Customer-provided context:
- Firewall team recently deployed strict egress policy that blocks UDP 53 and rate-limits ICMP to 1 pps per prefix for security.
- SD-WAN vendor recently enabled MSS clamping on some tunnels.

title: Timezone-normalized audit stream with deterministic event UUIDs and per-event HMAC for Sumo Logic ingest
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer requests a guaranteed-ordered, timezone-normalized NDJSON audit stream suitable for Sumo Logic HTTP source ingestion. Requirements: deterministic event UUIDs (so identical logical events produce identical ids across retries), per-event HMAC signature header with key-rotation guidance, and UTC canonicalization of timestamps. This is intended as evidence for an upcoming security audit and to support forensic replayability across their SIEM.

Impact:
- Greenline cannot complete their SIEM onboarding without a reproducible schema and an example ingest configuration.
- They require per-event HMAC verification for tamper-evidence and a signed sample dataset for audit submission.

Environment:
- Customer production account ingest (Greenline-prod) -> Redwood audit webhook -> Sumo Logic HTTP collector (us1.sumologic.com). Region: us-east-1.

Requested deliverables (customer-facing):
1) NDJSON field schema document (canonical field names, required/optional fields, types).
2) Deterministic event_id algorithm spec (namespace + monotonic counter + canonical timestamp) with pseudo-code.
3) Per-event HMAC header spec (header name, algorithm, canonical string-to-sign, example HMAC value, KMS rotation guidance).
4) Sumo Logic HTTP Source example config (headers, content-type, recommended batch sizes, retry semantics).
5) Small signed sample dataset (100 events) and a verification script the customer can run locally.
6) Minimal transformation examples (jq and a lightweight Node script) to map Redwood fields to Sumo-friendly fields.

Steps to reproduce / verification steps requested by customer:
1) Provide sample NDJSON file and collector config.
2) Instruct customer to POST sample to their Sumo HTTP source and verify arrival and order.
3) Run provided verification script to validate deterministic event_id collisions and HMAC signatures.
4) Confirm timestamps normalized to UTC and monotonic sequence where expected.

Acceptance criteria:
- Sumo Logic team confirms events arrive and are queryable with original event_id values preserved.
- Verification script returns PASS for HMAC and timestamp canonicalization.
- Documentation includes clear KMS rotation procedure and recommended retention for verification artifacts.

Logs / evidence provided by customer:
- Example of failed ingestion attempt (HTTP 400) with request/response payloads attached to initial message.
- Screenshot of Sumo showing partial parse failures (field extraction mismatches).

Notes on security posture requested:
- Customer explicitly requests HMAC using SHA-256 and wants guidance on rotating signing keys monthly with a signed key manifest.
- They want per-tenant envelope separation (tenant_id in both payload and envelope header).

title: VPCE HTTP/2 connection coalescing reuses host header causing presigned S3 URL 403s from private deploy
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer FinVault reported intermittent 403 errors when downloading model artifacts from presigned S3 URLs during model initialization in their Private deployment. Errors only occur in production behind the customer's VPC Endpoint (Interface VPCE) and are traced to HTTP/2 connection reuse/coalescing at the VPCE layer.

Impact:
- Model pulls for a subset of hosts intermittently fail with HTTP 403 (AccessDenied) despite presigned URL being valid for the request timestamp.
- Failures are non-deterministic: repeated curl downloads may succeed or fail depending on connection reuse.
- Causes failed model pulls during cold starts, resulting in occasional inference errors and longer retries for the customer.

Environment:
- Customer: FinVault Inc. (private deployment)
- Region: us-west-2
- Private control plane with interface VPC Endpoint configured to egress to S3 via a shared NAT/edge appliance
- Client stack: model-worker instances with libcurl/HTTP/2 enabled, ALPN negotiation present

Steps to reproduce (customer provided):
1. Generate a presigned S3 URL for a model shard (virtual-hosted-style bucketname.s3.us-west-2.amazonaws.com).
2. On a model-worker instance behind the VPCE, run: 
   curl -v --http2 "<presigned-url>" -o /tmp/shard || true
3. Repeat the curl several times in quick succession.
4. Observe that some requests return 200 and some 403 AccessDenied.

Observed behavior / diagnostics already collected:
- Server-side S3 logs (from customer) show requests with Host header equal to the *previous* virtual host on the same connection; signature verification fails because the signing host != Host header in some requests.
- Packet capture from the model-worker showed a persistent TLS connection to the VPCE with HTTP/2 multiplexed streams: stream N used Host header for bucket-a.example, stream N+1 carried a presigned URL for bucket-b.example but the HPACK-compressed Host header reused previous value due to connection-level coalescing at the VPCE (VPCE terminated TLS and multiplexed upstream to S3).
- Reproduced briefly in our lab infra by setting up an interface VPCE with HTTP/2 enabled and running concurrent curl downloads: intermittent 403s reproduced when connection reuse/multiplexing occurs.
- curl --http1.1 (disabling HTTP/2) consistently succeeds. curl --header "Host: <signed-host>" forces success even when connection is reused, confirming Host header mismatch is root cause.

Hypothesis / root cause:
The VPCE (or intermediate edge appliance) is terminating TLS and reusing HTTP/2 connections to upstream S3 endpoints across different virtual-hosted buckets. When requests are multiplexed on the same upstream connection, the preserved HPACK table or connection coalescing behavior can cause Host headers to be incorrectly associated with streams (or upstream connection rewrite removes per-request SNI/Host isolation), breaking SigV4 host-based signature validation for presigned URLs. In short: connection coalescing at the VPCE/edge layer is mixing Host/SNI context between multiplexed streams.

Temporary workarounds tried by customer and results:
- Forcing HTTP/1.1 from workers (curl --http1.1 or disable http2 in client) — Consistently works.
- Adding explicit Host header in client requests — Works for single-threaded fetch but not feasible at scale across SDKs without code changes.
- Creating path-style presigned URLs (s3.amazonaws.com/bucket/...) — Not accepted due to bucket naming policies and SDK constraints.

Investigation notes:
- VPCE configuration: interface endpoint configured with Private DNS enabled, security groups allow 443. No additional proxy was configured by Redwood. Customer has an edge NAT/SSL-offload appliance that sits between VPCE and public S3. That appliance appears to be terminating TLS and multiplexing HTTP/2 upstream.
- We validated that if VPCE/edge forwards TLS pass-through (no TLS termination), the issue does not reproduce.
- Captured HPACK frames show Host header indexed entries being reused across streams when the upstream terminator coalesces different hostnames on a single upstream connection.
- Related internal note: this is distinct from typical proxy header-injection issues; the failure is due to header compression/state reuse in HTTP/2 when upstream connection is shared across virtual hosts.

Proposed mitigations / next steps:
1) Short-term: advise FinVault to force HTTP/1.1 or disable client-side HTTP/2 connection reuse for model workers until the upstream coalescing is addressed (workaround documented).
2) Medium-term: recommend customer update edge appliance settings to preserve SNI and avoid coalescing multiple TLS hostnames over a single upstream connection (disable connection coalescing/connection coalescing by hostname).
3) Platform-side: update private-deploy runbook to include VPCE/edge validation checklist for HTTP/2 and TLS passthrough requirements for presigned URL workloads.
4) Long-term: evaluate whether our VPCE docs need a clear warning about intermediaries performing TLS termination+HTTP/2 multiplexing for S3-like virtual-hosted endpoints.

Resolution notes (work in progress):
- Customer accepted short-term workaround to pin curl/SDKs to HTTP/1.1 and roll patch to model-workers.
- SRE engaged with customer networking team to change edge appliance settings (pending customer change window).

Attachments/Artifacts:
- pcap snippet (redacted) showing HPACK index reuse (uploaded to ticket attachment).
- Customer-provided S3 access logs showing signature mismatch timestamps (uploaded).

title: cache-index-mismatch triggers cold KV lookups on repeated prompts causing timeouts
preview: Issue summary: BrightWrite Labs reports repeat prompts (identical user messages sent sequentially) are experiencing significantly higher latencies and occasional 504 timeouts starting 2026-03-12 08:30 UTC. Impact: production traffic for a dedicated tenant; customer reports degraded UX with repeat prompt cases in their chat product, estimated 18% of requests hitting the slow path for recent hour. Environment: Dedicated pool (dedicated-nyc-1), prod, us-east-1 region. Observed: p50 increased from ~60ms to ~320ms, p95 from ~220ms to ~1.8s for repeat prompts; error spikes of 504/timeouts at ~0.6% during peak. Immediate customer impact: user-facing failures and latency spikes for repeated-turn interactions (e.g., quick clarifications).
text: Issue summary: BrightWrite Labs reports repeat prompts (identical user messages sent sequentially) are experiencing significantly higher latencies and occasional 504 timeouts starting 2026-03-12 08:30 UTC. Impact: production traffic for a dedicated tenant; customer reports degraded UX with repeat prompt cases in their chat product, estimated 18% of requests hitting the slow path for recent hour. Environment: Dedicated pool (dedicated-nyc-1), prod, us-east-1 region. Observed: p50 increased from ~60ms to ~320ms, p95 from ~220ms to ~1.8s for repeat prompts; error spikes of 504/timeouts at ~0.6% during peak. Immediate customer impact: user-facing failures and latency spikes for repeated-turn interactions (e.g., quick clarifications).

title: Cleanup and reconcile SLA orchestration: template variables, routing keys, and reporting parity
preview:
Issue summary:
Recent deploys to sla-enforcer-v2 and the rule-engine refactor created several small divergences between SLA configuration, routing key normalization, and the downstream reporting transforms. The net effect: daily SLA breach counts in the support dashboard are ~7% lower than expected and some internal routing macros do not expand, causing tickets to be auto-assigned to the default queue instead of tiered on-call.

Impact:
- Incorrect auto-assignment for ~120 internal support tickets last week (internal queues).
- Daily SLA breach totals for internal dashboards are undercounted (~7% gap) which affects SRE on-call capacity planning.
- Risk of misrouted P1/P2 escalations if macros are not fixed before next release window.

Observed behavior / Evidence:
- Audit log from sla-enforcer (2026-03-08 03:14 - 03:46 UTC) shows macro expansion failures: variable \"priority_level\" not found; templates expect \"priority\".
- Rule-engine PR #1420 changed routing key normalization from kebab-case to snake_case; existing SLA configs reference kebab-case route keys (e.g., \"sla-high-latency\").
- Reporting pipeline transform (support-alerts consumer) drop observed for field \"sla_target_ms\" after schema evolution on Kafka topic support.alerts.v2 (see consumer offset gaps and schema registry diff).
- Slack thread: #internal-sla-tooling (2026-03-09) contains a reproduction trace and raw event samples.

Hypothesis / Root cause (working):
1) Template variable rename: a configuration migration in sla-enforcer-v2 introduced a canonical variable name \"priority_level\" while existing macros and templates used \"priority\"; the templating layer does not provide a backwards-compatibility alias.
2) Routing key normalization mismatch: rule-engine normalization change means configured routing rules no longer match incoming normalized keys unless configs are migrated.
3) Reporting transform schema drift: reporting consumer expects legacy schema and silently drops fields it doesn't find, producing undercounted metrics.

Immediate mitigation (short-term):
- Revert sla-enforcer template parsing to accept both \"priority\" and \"priority_level\" (hotfix branch prepared: support-tooling PR #1432).
- Add temporary adapter in rule-engine that canonicalizes incoming keys to both formats to avoid loss of matches (feature flag controlled).
- Patch reporting consumer to log dropped fields and emit counts to metrics for visibility while consumer is migrated.

Proposed long-term fix (plan):
- Full config migration script that rewrites existing routing keys to snake_case and updates macros to canonical variable names; add migration dry-run and rollback support.
- Add schema/version headers to support.alerts topics and upgrade reporting consumers with explicit schema evolution handling; add an alert when \"sla_target_ms\" is missing from >0.1% of messages.
- Add unit and integration tests that exercise macro expansion for both legacy and new templates and include these checks in the release pipeline.

Acceptance criteria:
- Macro expansion error rate drops to <0.01% for the next 7 days in prod.
- Routing miss rate due to key normalization drops to zero after migration (validated by matched/unmatched routing metrics).
- Daily SLA counts in dashboard reconcile to within 0.5% of ground-truth sampling (manual audit) for two consecutive days.

Next steps / Actions assigned:
- Mateo Alvarez: apply hotfix to sla-enforcer and enable feature flag for adapter in rule-engine (ETA 2026-03-12).
- Priya Mehta (Reporting): deploy patch to reporting consumer to surface dropped-field metrics and run backfill on 2026-03-11's topic window.
- Leah Patel: author config migration script and schedule maintenance window; create rollback plan and confluence doc.

Follow-ups / Observability playbook additions:
- Add a dedicated Grafana panel for template expansion error rate and quota for the support team.
- Create a runbook step to run the routing-key migration dry-run before any rule-engine normalization changes.
- Add a pre-deploy integration test that validates that the support console resolves macros against a canonical template set.

Priority and timeline rationale:
This is P2/Sev2 because the issue affects internal routing and metrics accuracy (operational visibility), but there is a straightforward hotfix and low immediate customer impact. The goal is to complete migration and validation before the next monthly deploy cycle.

title: WAF header rewrite causing subaccount quota attribution shift and unexpected 429s on private deployment
preview:
Issue summary:
Customer BrightCart (private deployment in eu-west) reported intermittent 429 throttles that started after a WAF policy change rolled out on their edge. The 429s are concentrated on a single subaccount and coincide with high background traffic from a third-party image-optimizer pipeline.

Impact:
- Several storefront ingestion workers began receiving 429s between 03:20-04:05 UTC on 2026-03-12, causing delayed catalog updates for ~12% of jobs.
- Customer reports intermittent user-facing slowness for product updates during the window.

Environment:
- Private deployment (customer-managed VPC) running Redwood private control plane (v1.8.3).
- Edge WAF sits in front of the customer ingress and applies custom header rewrites.

Observed behavior:
- Requests that previously attributed usage to distinct subaccounts have been attributed to a single subaccount id (subacct-9812) after the WAF rule change.
- Rate-limiter logs show token deductions against subacct-9812 for requests that include an X-Customer-Subacct header rewritten by the WAF into a header we use for subaccount mapping.

Expected behavior:
- WAF should not rewrite or drop the identity headers used for subaccount attribution, or the rewrite should preserve original values so per-subaccount quotas remain correct.

Notes:
- Customer rolled out a WAF rule to add an X-Forwarded-User header earlier the same morning. That rule also normalized Authorization headers for downstream routing. The normalization appears to have removed or replaced the original X-Customer-Subacct header in some flows.
text:
Issue summary:
Customer BrightCart (private deployment in eu-west) reported intermittent 429 throttles that started after a WAF policy change rolled out on their edge. The 429s are concentrated on a single subaccount and coincide with high background traffic from a third-party image-optimizer pipeline.

Impact:
- Several storefront ingestion workers began receiving 429s between 03:20-04:05 UTC on 2026-03-12, causing delayed catalog updates for ~12% of jobs.
- Customer reports intermittent user-facing slowness for product updates during the window.

Environment:
- Private deployment (customer-managed VPC) running Redwood private control plane (v1.8.3).
- Edge WAF sits in front of the customer ingress and applies custom header rewrites.

Observed behavior:
- Requests that previously attributed usage to distinct subaccounts have been attributed to a single subaccount id (subacct-9812) after the WAF rule change.
- Rate-limiter logs show token deductions against subacct-9812 for requests that include an X-Customer-Subacct header rewritten by the WAF into a header we use for subaccount mapping.

Expected behavior:
- WAF should not rewrite or drop the identity headers used for subaccount attribution, or the rewrite should preserve original values so per-subaccount quotas remain correct.

Notes:
- Customer rolled out a WAF rule to add an X-Forwarded-User header earlier the same morning. That rule also normalized Authorization headers for downstream routing. The normalization appears to have removed or replaced the original X-Customer-Subacct header in some flows.

title: Repeat-prompt latency spike (2-3x) after KV shard-mapping rollout; increased cold reads for prefix lookups
preview: Customer reports 2-3x higher tail latencies for repeated prompt requests in long-running chat sessions starting after 2026-03-09 04:00 UTC. Symptoms match cold KV/prefix lookups: prefix cache hit-rate dropped sharply leading to synchronous remote reads and request timeouts (504) for a subset of requests. Impact: Several hundred requests/minute for the customer's production org experienced >1s extra latency and intermittent timeouts; affecting their chat product's UX. The customer is on an enterprise Dedicated plan and is running production traffic through our us-east cluster where a KV shard-mapping change was rolled out in the morning of 2026-03-09.
text: Customer reports 2-3x higher tail latencies for repeated prompt requests in long-running chat sessions starting after 2026-03-09 04:00 UTC. Symptoms match cold KV/prefix lookups: prefix cache hit-rate dropped sharply leading to synchronous remote reads and request timeouts (504) for a subset of requests. Impact: Several hundred requests/minute for the customer's production org experienced >1s extra latency and intermittent timeouts; affecting their chat product's UX. The customer is on an enterprise Dedicated plan and is running production traffic through our us-east cluster where a KV shard-mapping change was rolled out in the morning of 2026-03-09.

title: Variant redispatch to secondary model during staged canary due to orchestrator snapshot lag
preview:
Issue summary: During a staged canary rollout for redwood-llama-7b-v2 in us-east, ~3% of BrightHealth AI traffic was unexpectedly routed to the secondary variant (quantized fallback) despite an explicit tenant model pin. Impact: customer reported mixed responses in production affecting 12 tenant sessions (chat flows). Symptoms observed at 09:21 UTC: inconsistent model outputs from the same session-id, and increased token latency (p50 -> p95).

High-level hypothesis: orchestrator snapshot application lag caused a partial control-plane commit where edge proxies had stale routing snapshots while the central router had already committed a canary weight update. This led to a redispatch path where requests that should have be pinned were re-evaluated by a reconciliation step and dispatched to the secondary variant.

Requested outcome: restore full pin coherence for affected tenant(s), identify root cause in orchestration snapshot propagation, and add guardrails to avoid redispatch during staged commits.

This led to a redispatch path where requests that should have be pinned were re-evaluated by a reconciliation step and dispatched to the secondary variant.

Requested outcome: restore full pin coherence for affected tenant(s), identify root cause in orchestration snapshot propagation, and add guardrails to avoid redispatch during staged commits.

title: Runner secret-cache livelock during bulk key rewrap blocks release cutover
preview:
Issue summary:
During a scheduled bulk KMS rewrap for ephemeral runner credentials, a subset of runners entered a secret-cache livelock where vault-sync repeatedly refreshed secrets but the runner-provisioner refused to mark nodes healthy. This prevented the release cutover job from acquiring the required pool of labeled runners and blocked the production deploy window.

Impact:
- Release cutover delayed for two release pipelines (nightly + hotfix).
- ~18% of ephemeral runner fleet in us-west-2 and 12% in eu-central-1 continuously flapped between REGISTERED and UNHEALTHY, failing job assignment.
- Some long-running jobs were evicted and re-queued, creating billing churn.

Environment:
- prod account (redwood-prod) running 2026-03-10 KMS rewrap job initiated from operations-rotate-key pipeline.
- Runner image v2026.02.1, runner-provisioner v1.14.0, vault-sync v0.9.3.
- Autoscaler policy: conservative-surge with max-unavailable 10%.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Triggered bulk KMS rewrap for ephemeral runner service account keys across regions.
2) vault-sync pushed new wrapped secrets and incremented secret-version labels on k8s Secret objects.
3) Runner pods attempted to fetch new secret; cache layer (runner-provisioner cache) attempted a soft-refresh but observed nonce mismatch with authz-cache table.
4) Provisioner flagged pod as unhealthy and restarted the token renewal flow. vault-sync simultaneously rewrapped again due to perceived failure, renewing secret-version and causing the runner to see a new version mid-refresh.
5) Loop repeated until operator intervention.

Logs / Observations:
- Vault-sync logs (us-west-2): many 2026-03-10T22:14:xx entries "secret rewrap: success version=222->223" followed quickly by retries for same pod IDs.
- Runner-provisioner logs: repeated "cache nonce mismatch, dropping token refresh" followed by "marking runner UNHEALTHY: token-refresh-failed" for same container IDs.
- Authz-cache (redis) had transient high CPU and eviction spikes coincident with the rewrap window.
- Scheduler metrics showed job-assign latency spike to 4.1s (baseline 0.6s) and pending-job queue growth.

Investigation notes:
- Hypothesis: concurrent rewraps + eventual consistency of authz-cache caused the provisioner to observe mixed secret-versions across components and treat that as compromise, triggering aggressive eviction.
- Vault-sync retry/backoff is too eager when secrets appear outdated; it will rewrap again for a pod perceived as failed, which escalates version churn.
- The provisioner cache validates both secret-version and nonce; if versions do not align within a short window, it refuses to accept the token and evicts. The short acceptance window is too tight for our multi-region rewrap choreography.
- Related: similar pattern to INT-30542 (runner deregistered during credentials rotation) but here it manifests as livelock due to compounded rewrap retries across components.

Temporary workaround implemented:
- Paused the rolling rewrap in eu-central-1 and set vault-sync to single-threaded mode for us-west-2.
- Increased provisioner token acceptance window (config toggle) from 5s to 45s as an emergency mitigation to allow reconciliation.
- Manually reconciled ~120 affected pods by forcing a single rewrap and restarting runner-provisioner for the affected node pool.

Resolution plan (short-term):
- Stabilize by rolling a hotfix to vault-sync to add optimistic backoff and a per-pod rewrap gate so we don't rewrap the same pod while a previous refresh is in-flight. Target: vault-sync v0.9.4.
- Roll hotfix to runner-provisioner to make cache-version acceptance window configurable and to add a one-time idempotency token check to tolerate brief version skew. Target: v1.14.1.
- Increase authz-cache resource requests and add Redis persistence tuning to avoid eviction during rotation windows.
- Re-run controlled rotation with feature flags enabling single-region stagger and observability hooks.

Follow-ups and postmortem items:
- Postmortem to be authored once stable with timeline, blame-free root cause, and remediation owner assignments.
- Add automated test that simulates concurrent rewrap + cache skew to CI test harness.
- Update the Release Cutover Checklist to include a secret-rotation readiness gate.
- Consider orchestration change: central coordinator that stages rewraps per runner label instead of region-wide parallel rewraps.
text:

- Increase authz-cache resource requests and add Redis persistence tuning to avoid eviction during rotation windows.

title: High attach latency and throughput collapse from small-chunk inference stream CQ saturation
preview:
Issue summary: BrightWrite (dedicated pool) reported a mid-morning collapse in steady-state throughput for multiple batched routes. Impact: p50/p95 token throughput drop ~40-70% for routed models and p99 attach latency spiking to 1.2s from baseline ~80ms. Customer-facing inference requests saw elevated 5xx attach timeouts and increased fallbacks to smaller model variants. Environment: prod us-west dedicated pool, heterogeneous GPU hosts (A100 80GB + A10 mixed), customer traffic characterized by many short inference streams (~12-32 tokens) at high qps.

We observed a pattern inconsistent with normal autoscaler signals: autoscaler did not trigger additional hosts despite attach latency rising. Support triaged as potential noisy-tenant + kernel-level IO backpressure (PCIe completion queue saturation) affecting attach path and the serving scheduler fairness.

Impact details: Customer reported 30% drop in successful completions during business-hour window, increased errors correlated with sudden bursts of thousands of small requests per second.

Runbook links above for quick reference.

Runbook links above for quick reference.

title: Post-migration edge proxy: intermittent 429s for mixed API keys and signed-url callbacks
preview:
Issue summary:
After a scheduled edge proxy migration (blue->green) on 2026-03-09, the customer reports intermittent 429 responses for a subset of traffic that uses a mix of persistent API keys and signed URL callbacks. Impact: several ETL workers and webhook callbacks saw periodic failures for ~20 minutes during a heavy ingest window, causing backlog and retries downstream.

Impact:
- Acme Analytics batch ingest pipeline experienced ~18% failed request rate during the window, causing requeues and delayed downstream processing.
- Customer reported billing and operational disruption; they are on a Dedicated plan and requested immediate assistance.

Environment:
- Customer Dedicated pool in us-west-2, routed through new edge-proxy cluster (ep-usw-3).
- Mix of long-lived API keys (service accounts) and short-lived signed URLs generated by their ingestion service.
- HTTP/2 multiplexing between edge and origin observed in traces.

Steps to reproduce (customer-provided):
1) Generate a signed-upload URL from the customer app.
2) Start 40 parallel uploads using both signed URLs and API-key-authenticated PUTs to the same tenant.
3) Observe intermittent 429s on ~1 in 20 requests during high concurrency.

Observed behavior:
- 429s return with x-rate-limit headers showing token window at 0 for tenant-level bucket.
- Many 429s appear correlated with short bursts of TCP connection resets during proxy cutover.
- Some successful requests share identical Authorization header value but different socket metadata (client IP/port).

Expected behavior:
- Mixed authentication flows should be accounted properly and not double-count into the same short-window burst unless tenant capacity is exhausted.

Logs/headers attached by customer (truncated):
- x-rate-limit-tenant: 0/300
- x-rate-limit-window: 10s
- Authorization: Bearer sk-prod-ACME-xxx
- Signed-URL: X-Amz-SignedHeaders...

Notes on severity:
- P1/Sev1 because production ingestion is degraded and customer is on Dedicated pool with SLA expectations.

title: Batcher synchronization glitch causing periodic p95/p99 latency surges and intermittent 504s for short streaming requests
preview: Issue summary: Over the last 48 hours NimbleDocs (dedicated customer) has reported recurring p95/p99 latency spikes and a small number of 504 timeouts affecting short streaming requests. These occur in bursts approximately every 5-8 minutes and correlate with a rise in batcher flush latency on a subset of nodes. Impact: Customer reports degraded UX for their chat widget during peak hours; some requests hit 504 and are retried by clients. Frequency: spikes observed ~6–12x per hour during the incident window. Initial hypothesis: a synchronization edge-case in the continuous batcher interacting with KV cache warmup/prefetch locks under low-concurrency short-stream traffic.
text: Issue summary: Over the last 48 hours NimbleDocs (dedicated customer) has reported recurring p95/p99 latency spikes and a small number of 504 timeouts affecting short streaming requests. These occur in bursts approximately every 5-8 minutes and correlate with a rise in batcher flush latency on a subset of nodes. Impact: Customer reports degraded UX for their chat widget during peak hours; some requests hit 504 and are retried by clients. Frequency: spikes observed ~6–12x per hour during the incident window. Initial hypothesis: a synchronization edge-case in the continuous batcher interacting with KV cache warmup/prefetch locks under low-concurrency short-stream traffic.

title: Midday throughput cliff on dedicated capacity traced to silent-tenant heartbeat suppression causing allocator queue blocking and autoscaler stall
preview:
Issue summary:
During a midday traffic window (approx 12:45–13:15 UTC) a FinNova dedicated capacity cluster experienced a sharp drop in successful throughput for batched routes serving redwood-rlhf-13b. P50 remained acceptable but p95/p99 spiked and many requests were queued >10s. Customers reported timeouts and increased 5xx rate.

Impact:
- FinNova observed ~40% throughput loss for critical batching routes for ~24 minutes.
- Several requests hit route-level timeouts and triggered client retries.
- No billing or data-loss impact observed.

Environment:
- Dedicated pool: finnova-prod-1 (reserved)
- Region: us-east-1
- Models: redwood-rlhf-13b (pinned v2026-02-28), fallback to open-llama-13b-v2 enabled

Steps to reproduce (as observed):
1) Targeted load pattern: many small interactive streams (~200–300 simultaneous short streams) started after a quiet period.
2) Autoscaler did not provision additional GPU attachments in expected window; allocator queue showed a buildup.
3) Inflight attach requests were blocked behind a set of hung leases where heartbeats from a low-traffic tenant were being suppressed (no obvious CPU/GPU saturation).

Observed behavior:
- Autoscaler metrics showed low demand signal preceding the incident, then a sudden spike; autoscaler decision loop returned "no-scale" for several evaluation cycles due to stale telemetry from allocator.
- Allocator queue length increased; the first-in-line attachments remained pending because a small group of sockets associated with a quiet tenant had suppressed heartbeats and held ephemeral slot reservations.
- Prefetch and batching subsystems could not drain; per-gpu slot usage reported a small number of slots at >99% reservation while overall utilization stayed moderate.

Expected behavior:
- Allocator should time out suppressed heartbeats/leases quickly and allow new attachments to use slots.
- Autoscaler should react to sudden spike by scaling up a minimal number of GPUs or triggering fallback routing to lower-cost variants before p99 latency increases.

Logs / dashboards referenced:
- Dashboard: https://grafana.redwood.local/d/fin-nova/dedicated-pool-overview?var=pool=finnova-prod-1
- Allocator trace: traces.redwood.local/alloc/500321 (internal link)
- Slack thread: #support-finnova (ts 2026-03-10 12:56 UTC)
- Related incident on status page: INC-2026-0310-01

Initial hypothesis:
- A silent/quiet tenant with sporadic long-lived socket sessions suppressed heartbeats from sidecar, holding ephemeral slot reservations. This caused a head-of-line blocking in the allocator queue. Autoscaler inputs were skewed by delayed telemetry and did not scale in time. Noise was not the typical CPU/GPU saturation signal but reservation-level interference (noisy-neighbor-like).

title: Scheduled alert digest emails strip tracehook query params with leading underscore after retention rollover
preview:
Issue summary: Several customers (first reported by Nimbus Analytics) have reported that scheduled alert digest emails generated right after a retention rollover strip specific tracehook query parameters that begin with an underscore (e.g., _trace_id, _route).

Impact: Digests contain links that no longer include the required tracehook params. When users click the link from the digest the console loads but the trace context is missing, so the alert does not link to the originating trace. This breaks escalation workflows and increases investigation time for on-call engineers.

Environment: Production, runs during nightly retention rollovers and compact windows in us-east.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Have an org with scheduled alert digests configured to include tracehook links (example org: Nimbus Analytics).
2) Ensure retention compaction is scheduled to run within the same hour as the digest generation window.
3) Trigger a scheduled digest during retention rollover (or simulate with retention flag in staging).
4) Open the digest email and click the embedded tracehook link.

Expected behavior: Links in the digest retain all query parameters (including internal/underscored params) so clicking jumps to the trace with full context and tracehook anchor.

Actual behavior: Links in the digest are sanitized: parameters beginning with underscore are removed and in some cases the entire query string is stripped. Resulting links open the dashboard but lose anchor/time/route parameters.

Notes: This appears tied to the digest HTML generation path that runs a sanitizer step after we materialize query strings from the scheduled-report worker. The sanitizer was hardened recently to remove potentially unsafe params; however this change should not remove internal tracehook keys. The issue first surfaced after the retention compaction change that adds a short-lived metadata rewrite during rollover (suspected race between compaction metadata and digest worker).
text:
Issue summary: Several customers (first reported by Nimbus Analytics) have reported that scheduled alert digest emails generated right after a retention rollover strip specific tracehook query parameters that begin with an underscore (e.g., _trace_id, _route).

Impact: Digests contain links that no longer include the required tracehook params. When users click the link from the digest the console loads but the trace context is missing, so the alert does not link to the originating trace. This breaks escalation workflows and increases investigation time for on-call engineers.

Environment: Production, runs during nightly retention rollovers and compact windows in us-east.

Steps to reproduce:
1) Have an org with scheduled alert digests configured to include tracehook links (example org: Nimbus Analytics).
2) Ensure retention compaction is scheduled to run within the same hour as the digest generation window.
3) Trigger a scheduled digest during retention rollover (or simulate with retention flag in staging).
4) Open the digest email and click the embedded tracehook link.

Expected behavior: Links in the digest retain all query parameters (including internal/underscored params) so clicking jumps to the trace with full context and tracehook anchor.

Actual behavior: Links in the digest are sanitized: parameters beginning with underscore are removed and in some cases the entire query string is stripped. Resulting links open the dashboard but lose anchor/time/route parameters.

Notes: This appears tied to the digest HTML generation path that runs a sanitizer step after we materialize query strings from the scheduled-report worker. The sanitizer was hardened recently to remove potentially unsafe params; however this change should not remove internal tracehook keys. The issue first surfaced after the retention compaction change that adds a short-lived metadata rewrite during rollover (suspected race between compaction metadata and digest worker).

title: Sidecar connection thundering causing reserved-pool slot latch & transient throughput collapse
preview:
Issue summary: Between 2026-03-10 12:07 and 12:35 UTC, a customer (DataMuse - dedicated pool) observed a sharp drop in sustained throughput and elevated p99 latency on batched generation routes. Impact: multiple short-lived requests from many clients produced a sidecar connection storm which led to transient GPU slot reservations ('slot latch') and prefetch starvation for batched streams; customer reported 40-60% reduction in tokens/sec for 20 minutes. Environment: production reserved pool in us-east; Dedicated capacity cluster: rp-us-east-07; Serving runtime v2.8.1, sidecar-proxy v1.5.3, scheduler v0.9.4.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Customer traffic pattern: thousands of very short streams (median duration ~150ms) originating from a fanout of ~200 client endpoints hitting the same model route.
2) Sidecar proxies opened and closed connections rapidly; many connections reset mid-handshake.
3) Scheduler observed many transient slot allocations marked in-use while sidecar tracked heartbeat state; prefetch jobs queued but did not complete due to slot latch timing out.
4) Autoscaler did not trigger immediate scale-up (thresholds tuned to sustained queue depth) -> reserved pool maintained same slot count and p99 drift persisted.

Logs / snippets (sanitized):
- serving-runtime: "[12:12:17Z] slot-reserve: id=slot-78f, state=HELD_BY_SIDECAR, lease=30s"
- sidecar-proxy: "[12:12:18Z] conn: accept fd=210 remote=10.3.99.42:54321 state=ESTABLISHED -> RESET"
- scheduler: "[12:12:19Z] prefetch: id=pref-412 queued (dep: slot-78f) waiting for attach"
- k8s events: repeated CrashLoopBackOff on sidecar-reporter (metric handshake spikes)

Observed metrics: sudden spike in short-lived connections (+1200/min), slot_hold_count rose from baseline 4 -> 24, prefetch_queue_growth to 600, effective batch size dropped 3x, p99 latency increased from 180ms -> 1.4s for the route.
text:
Issue summary: Between 2026-03-10 12:07 and 12:35 UTC, a customer (DataMuse - dedicated pool) observed a sharp drop in sustained throughput and elevated p99 latency on batched generation routes. Impact: multiple short-lived requests from many clients produced a sidecar connection storm which led to transient GPU slot reservations ('slot latch') and prefetch starvation for batched streams; customer reported 40-60% reduction in tokens/sec for 20 minutes. Environment: production reserved pool in us-east; Dedicated capacity cluster: rp-us-east-07; Serving runtime v2.8.1, sidecar-proxy v1.5.3, scheduler v0.9.4.

Steps to reproduce (observed):
1) Customer traffic pattern: thousands of very short streams (median duration ~150ms) originating from a fanout of ~200 client endpoints hitting the same model route.
2) Sidecar proxies opened and closed connections rapidly; many connections reset mid-handshake.
3) Scheduler observed many transient slot allocations marked in-use while sidecar tracked heartbeat state; prefetch jobs queued but did not complete due to slot latch timing out.
4) Autoscaler did not trigger immediate scale-up (thresholds tuned to sustained queue depth) -> reserved pool maintained same slot count and p99 drift persisted.

Logs / snippets (sanitized):
- serving-runtime: "[12:12:17Z] slot-reserve: id=slot-78f, state=HELD_BY_SIDECAR, lease=30s"
- sidecar-proxy: "[12:12:18Z] conn: accept fd=210 remote=10.3.99.42:54321 state=ESTABLISHED -> RESET"
- scheduler: "[12:12:19Z] prefetch: id=pref-412 queued (dep: slot-78f) waiting for attach"
- k8s events: repeated CrashLoopBackOff on sidecar-reporter (metric handshake spikes)

Observed metrics: sudden spike in short-lived connections (+1200/min), slot_hold_count rose from baseline 4 -> 24, prefetch_queue_growth to 600, effective batch size dropped 3x, p99 latency increased from 180ms -> 1.4s for the route.

title: Mesh routing flap in edge fabric causing extended TLS handshake and request timeouts for us-west customers
preview:
Issue summary:
Customers in the us-west region are observing elevated request latencies and sporadic request timeouts. The symptoms trace to an edge mesh routing flap that produces prolonged TLS handshake duration and occasional connection resets when the gateway picks a cross-region egress path.

Impact:
- Multiple Clipstitch Media endpoints experienced 5-10% request failures (timeouts) during the window; p95 increased from ~120ms to ~2.1s for text generation calls.
- Affected features: streaming responses sometimes disconnect mid-stream; non-streaming generation requests hit 504s after backend timeout.

Environment:
- Prod customers in us-west region on Dedicated pools
- API endpoints: /v1/generate, /v1/stream
- Proxy layer: edge-nginx mesh with smart-routing enabled

Observed behavior:
1) Client establishes TCP but TLS handshake time increases unpredictably (handshake durations sampling at 800ms-1.8s).
2) When handshake times exceed backend connect timeout, gateway returns 504 or terminates stream.
3) Traces show egress chosen intermittently to eu-west peering path instead of local direct egress; that path has known higher RTT and transient packet reordering.

Expected behavior:
- Edge mesh should prefer local direct egress to minimize handshake latency and should not select cross-region egress for low-latency traffic unless local path is degraded per policy.

Steps to reproduce:
1) From a us-west client simulate 1000 rapid generation requests to /v1/generate with default timeouts.
2) Observe p95/p99 and TLS handshake metrics on the request traces.
3) Re-run while toggling smart-routing policy to force-local vs default to see delta.

Additional notes:
- Customer provided request IDs and timestamps in logs below.
- Issue appears correlated with a mesh control-plane event at approx 2026-03-10T02:14:00Z (control-plane roll for route weights).
text:
Issue summary:
Customers in the us-west region are observing elevated request latencies and sporadic request timeouts. The symptoms trace to an edge mesh routing flap that produces prolonged TLS handshake duration and occasional connection resets when the gateway picks a cross-region egress path.

Impact:

- Affected features: streaming responses sometimes disconnect mid-stream; non-streaming generation requests hit 504s after backend timeout.

Environment:
- Prod customers in us-west region on Dedicated pools
- API endpoints: /v1/generate, /v1/stream
- Proxy layer: edge-nginx mesh with smart-routing enabled

Observed behavior:
1) Client establishes TCP but TLS handshake time increases unpredictably (handshake durations sampling at 800ms-1.8s).
2) When handshake times exceed backend connect timeout, gateway returns 504 or terminates stream.
3) Traces show egress chosen intermittently to eu-west peering path instead of local direct egress; that path has known higher RTT and transient packet reordering.

Expected behavior:
- Edge mesh should prefer local direct egress to minimize handshake latency and should not select cross-region egress for low-latency traffic unless local path is degraded per policy.

Steps to reproduce:

2) Observe p95/p99 and TLS handshake metrics on the request traces.
3) Re-run while toggling smart-routing policy to force-local vs default to see delta.

Additional notes:
- Customer provided request IDs and timestamps in logs below.
- Issue appears correlated with a mesh control-plane event at approx 2026-03-10T02:14:00Z (control-plane roll for route weights).

title: SAML assertions signed with RSA-PSS rejected by private auth proxy causing 403s for SSO logins
preview:
Issue summary: Customer reports that users in their private deployment cannot complete SSO login. Requests to our auth proxy return 403 Authorization Denied.
Impact: All Aurelia Finance users in the eu-west private deployment are unable to sign in via SSO (affects web console and API token exchange for automation).
Environment: Private deployment (customer-managed control plane) running auth-proxy v1.8.1, auth-service v3.2.0. IdP: corporate SAML IdP configured to sign assertions with RSA-PSS (RSASSA-PSS with SHA256).
Observed behaviour: SAML assertion accepted by IdP and delivered to our endpoint, but auth-proxy logs show signature validation failure and downstream RBAC mapping is not performed, resulting in 403.
Expected behaviour: Our SAML verifier should validate IdP-signed assertions and proceed to role mapping; if algorithm unsupported, we should surface clear diagnostic and provide a compatibility path.
text:
Issue summary: Customer reports that users in their private deployment cannot complete SSO login. Requests to our auth proxy return 403 Authorization Denied.
Impact: All Aurelia Finance users in the eu-west private deployment are unable to sign in via SSO (affects web console and API token exchange for automation).
Environment: Private deployment (customer-managed control plane) running auth-proxy v1.8.1, auth-service v3.2.0. IdP: corporate SAML IdP configured to sign assertions with RSA-PSS (RSASSA-PSS with SHA256).
Observed behaviour: SAML assertion accepted by IdP and delivered to our endpoint, but auth-proxy logs show signature validation failure and downstream RBAC mapping is not performed, resulting in 403.
Expected behaviour: Our SAML verifier should validate IdP-signed assertions and proceed to role mapping; if algorithm unsupported, we should surface clear diagnostic and provide a compatibility path.

title: NVMe disk signature reorder after depot repair causes Intune/assetdb mismatch and Autopilot hostname collision
preview: Issue summary: After depot repair (NVMe replacement or reflow) some devices boot with the OS disk signature and partition GUIDs reordered. This triggers our assetdb/device fingerprinting logic to report a different Device ID back to Intune/Autopilot. Impact: devices appear as duplicate assets, Autopilot hostnames collide (hostname-1, hostname-1-1), and deployment policies targeted by device ID do not apply. Scope observed: ~7 devices from the same repair batch over the last two weeks. Not observed in fresh builds from imaging lab.
text: Issue summary: After depot repair (NVMe replacement or reflow) some devices boot with the OS disk signature and partition GUIDs reordered. This triggers our assetdb/device fingerprinting logic to report a different Device ID back to Intune/Autopilot. Impact: devices appear as duplicate assets, Autopilot hostnames collide (hostname-1, hostname-1-1), and deployment policies targeted by device ID do not apply. Scope observed: ~7 devices from the same repair batch over the last two weeks. Not observed in fresh builds from imaging lab.
